content.content:
1→# Architecture
     2→
     3→> This is the authoritative source for the current architecture of the Todo List application.
     4→> For the rationale behind each decision, see the ADR PRs labeled `adr` on GitHub.
     5→
     6→## Technology Stack
     7→
     8→### Event Sourcing Framework: Commanded
     9→
    10→The application uses [Commanded](https://github.com/commanded/commanded) as its
    11→CQRS/ES framework. Commanded provides the full command dispatch pipeline,
    12→aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection infrastructure.
    13→
    14→Key components from the Commanded ecosystem:
    15→
    16→- **commanded** -- Core framework (command router, aggregate callbacks, middleware)
    17→- **commanded_eventstore_adapter** -- Bridges Commanded to the EventStore backend
    18→- **commanded_ecto_projections** -- Ecto-based read model projections
    19→- **commanded_boilerplate** -- Ergonomic macros for aggregate-per-command pattern
    20→  (GitHub: `jwilger/commanded_boilerplate`)
    21→
    22→See "Domain Architecture" below for how commands and aggregates are structured.
    23→
    24→### Event Store Backend: PostgreSQL (EventStore) + In-Memory for Tests
    25→
    26→Events are durably stored in PostgreSQL using the
    27→[eventstore](https://github.com/commanded/eventstore) library with the
    28→`commanded_eventstore_adapter`. This requires a dedicated PostgreSQL database
    29→separate from the main Ecto application database.
    30→
    31→**Two PostgreSQL databases** (same docker-compose instance):
    32→
    33→- **Application database** (Ecto) -- Projections / read models, standard Phoenix data
    34→- **Event store database** (EventStore) -- Append-only event streams
    35→
    36→**Test environment:** Tests use Commanded's built-in in-memory event store adapter.
    37→This keeps tests fast (no database I/O), isolated (no shared state between tests),
    38→and free of infrastructure dependencies.
    39→
    40→### Projection Mechanism: commanded_ecto_projections
    41→
    42→Read models are built using
    43→[commanded_ecto_projections](https://github.com/commanded/commanded-ecto-projections).
    44→Each read model has a corresponding Projector module that uses
    45→`Commanded.Projections.Ecto` and implements `project/2` callbacks for each event
    46→type it handles.
    47→
    48→**Two projectors:**
    49→
    50→- **TaskProjector** -- Projects events into an Ecto schema used by both the
    51→  ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList read models
    52→
    53→Projection tables live in the **application database** (Ecto), not the event store
    54→database. The library handles:
    55→
    56→- **Idempotency** -- Tracks which events have been processed, preventing duplicates
    57→- **Error handling** -- Provides configurable error/retry behaviour
    58→- **Subscription management** -- Integrates with Commanded's event subscription system
    59→
    60→### UUID Generation: UUIDv7 (uuidv7 hex package)
    61→
    62→Task identifiers use UUID v7 via the
    63→[uuidv7](https://hex.pm/packages/uuidv7) hex package (v1.0.0). UUID v7
    64→(RFC 9562) embeds a Unix timestamp in the high bits, producing identifiers that
    65→are monotonically increasing and time-sortable while remaining globally unique.
    66→
    67→The package includes an `UUIDv7` Ecto type for seamless schema integration.
    68→
    69→**Generation strategy:** Task IDs are generated by the context module
    70→(`TodoList.Tasks`) and passed on the command struct. The aggregate does not
    71→generate IDs internally. This design enables tests to supply deterministic IDs
    72→without mocking.
    73→
    74→## Domain Architecture
    75→
    76→### Aggregate Design: Aggregate-per-Command with commanded_boilerplate
    77→
    78→The application uses the **aggregate-per-command** pattern via the
    79→[commanded_boilerplate](https://github.com/jwilger/commanded_boilerplate) library.
    80→Each command module defines its own inline aggregate containing **only** the state
    81→fields needed for that specific command's decision-making. There is no shared
    82→"Task" aggregate module.
    83→
    84→**Key aspects:**
    85→
    86→- `use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command, identifier: :task_id` -- Sets up the command
    87→  module with routing, validation middleware, and authorization middleware
    88→- `inputs do ... end` -- Defines the command's input fields as a typed struct
    89→- `aggregate do ... end` -- Defines the command's inline aggregate with only
    90→  relevant fields and `apply/2` callbacks for event application
    91→- `handle/2` -- The command handler callback that receives aggregate state and
    92→  command, returns events or errors
    93→- `register_command/2` -- Auto-registers the command with Commanded's router
    94→
    95→**Domain logic** lives directly in the command module's `handle/2` callback.
    96→There is no separate domain layer and no `lib/todo_list/domain/` directory.
    97→
    98→**Aggregate lifecycle:** `DefaultLifespan` stops aggregate processes immediately
    99→after command execution (reliability over performance). Each command execution
   100→loads only the state it needs.
   101→
   102→**Authorization:** The boilerplate includes authorization middleware. For this
   103→single-user app with no authentication, the `authorize/2` callback returns `:ok`
   104→unconditionally.
   105→
   106→**Read model queries:** The boilerplate also provides `Commanded.Boilerplate.Query`
   107→for read model access with validation and Repo integration.
   108→
   109→**Maintenance:** The `commanded_boilerplate` library is owned by the project
   110→maintainer. If compatibility issues arise with newer Commanded versions, it can be
   111→cloned locally at `../commanded_boilerplate/` for updates.
   112→
   113→### Domain Types: Semantic Types via newt
   114→
   115→The application uses **dedicated semantic domain types** built with the
   116→[newt](https://hex.pm/packages/newt) library (v9.0.0). Newt is already a
   117→transitive dependency via `commanded_boilerplate`; the library is owned by the
   118→project maintainer (GitHub: `jwilger/newt`).
   119→
   120→**Naming convention -- domain-semantic, not structural:** Types are named after
   121→what they represent in the domain, not their structural shape.
   122→
   123→- `TodoList.TaskDescription` -- wraps `String.t()`, validates non-empty /
   124→  non-blank, trims whitespace
   125→- `TodoList.TaskId` -- wraps `String.t()`, validates UUIDv7 format
   126→- `TodoList.TaskPosition` -- wraps `integer()`, validates positive integer
   127→
   128→**How newt works:**
   129→
   130→- `use Newt, type: <inner_type>` -- Creates a wrapped struct with `new/1`,
   131→  `new!/1`, `unwrap/1`
   132→- `validate/1` callback -- Override to add domain-specific validation logic
   133→- Auto-generated `Ectotype` module (e.g., `TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype`)
   134→  implementing `Ecto.Type` for seamless Ecto schema integration
   135→- Protocol implementations for `Jason`, `Phoenix.Param`, `Phoenix.HTML.Safe`,
   136→  `Inspect`, `String.Chars`
   137→- `type!/2` guard macro for pattern matching
   138→
   139→**Parse at boundaries, trust internally:** Raw strings and integers enter the
   140→system at command boundaries (e.g., from LiveView forms). Commands parse them
   141→into domain types using `new/1`. Once parsed, domain types flow through events,
   142→aggregates, and projections without re-validation. This ensures invalid data
   143→never propagates past the boundary.
   144→
   145→**Ecto integration:** Each newt type auto-generates an `Ectotype` module that
   146→implements `Ecto.Type`, so projection schemas use field types like
   147→`TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype` directly.
   148→
   149→**Maintenance:** The `newt` library is owned by the project maintainer. If
   150→issues arise, it can be cloned locally at `../newt/` for updates.
   151→
   152→### Context Module API: TodoList.Tasks
   153→
   154→The context module `TodoList.Tasks` is the **single API boundary** between the
   155→web layer (LiveView) and the domain layer (commands, aggregates, projections).
   156→LiveView ONLY talks to the context module -- never to Commanded directly.
   157→
   158→**Command functions** wrap command dispatch. Each function constructs the command
   159→struct, dispatches it via Commanded, and returns a clean result:
   160→
   161→- `add_task(description)` -- Generates a `TaskId` via UUIDv7, builds an
   162→  `AddTask` command, dispatches it. Returns `{:ok, task_id}` or
   163→  `{:error, reason}`.
   164→- `complete_task(task_id)` -- Returns `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`.
   165→- `reopen_task(task_id)` -- Returns `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`.
   166→- `change_task_description(task_id, new_description)` -- Returns `:ok` or
   167→  `{:error, reason}`.
   168→- `reprioritize_task(task_id, new_position)` -- Returns `:ok` or
   169→  `{:error, reason}`.
   170→- `delete_task(task_id)` -- Returns `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`.
   171→
   172→**Query functions** wrap read model access using
   173→`Commanded.Boilerplate.Query` internally:
   174→
   175→- `active_tasks()` -- Returns the list of active (non-completed) tasks ordered
   176→  by position.
   177→- `completed_tasks()` -- Returns the list of completed tasks ordered by
   178→  completion time descending.
   179→
   180→**Responsibilities of the context module:**
   181→
   182→- **UUID generation** -- Creates `TaskId` values via UUIDv7 for new tasks
   183→- **Command construction** -- Builds command structs from function arguments
   184→- **Command dispatch** -- Sends commands through Commanded's pipeline
   185→- **Response transformation** -- Converts Commanded dispatch results into clean
   186→  `{:ok, result}` / `{:error, reason}` tuples
   187→- **Query execution** -- Fetches projection data via the boilerplate's Query
   188→  module
   189→
   190→**Testing benefit:** LiveView tests can mock or stub the context module rather
   191→than mocking Commanded internals, keeping integration tests focused on UI
   192→behaviour.
   193→
   194→## Integration Patterns
   195→
   196→### LiveView Integration: Streams + Projection Subscriptions (Hybrid)
   197→
   198→LiveView uses a **hybrid** approach: **streams** for the main task lists and
   199→**direct reads** for one-off queries or detail views.
   200→
   201→**Streams for lists:** The ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList are rendered
   202→using LiveView's `stream/3`. Streams efficiently track insertions, updates, and
   203→deletions -- only changed items are sent to the client.
   204→
   205→- Parent elements use `phx-update="stream"`
   206→- Each streamed item has a DOM ID (e.g., `"tasks-#{task.id}"`)
   207→- Streams are **not enumerable** -- empty states and counts use separate assigns
   208→
   209→**Projection subscriptions for real-time updates:** Projectors broadcast
   210→changes via Phoenix PubSub after processing events. LiveView subscribes to
   211→these broadcasts on mount and updates streams accordingly.
   212→
   213→**Lifecycle:**
   214→
   215→1. **Mount:** Subscribe to projection update topics via Phoenix PubSub; stream
   216→   initial data from `TodoList.Tasks.active_tasks()` or
   217→   `TodoList.Tasks.completed_tasks()`
   218→2. **User action:** Event handler calls a context command function (e.g.,
   219→   `TodoList.Tasks.complete_task(task_id)`)
   220→3. **Projection update:** The projector processes the resulting event, updates
   221→   the database, and broadcasts via PubSub
   222→4. **Stream update:** LiveView receives the PubSub message in `handle_info/2`
   223→   and calls `stream_insert/4`, `stream_delete/3`, etc.
   224→
   225→**Key details:**
   226→
   227→- Commands dispatch through the context module (never directly from LiveView)
   228→- The LiveView does **not** re-fetch the full list after each command; the
   229→  PubSub subscription delivers granular updates
   230→- For empty states and aggregate counts (e.g., `completed_count`), use separate
   231→  assigns updated alongside stream operations
   232→- This is a single-user app, but the subscription pattern keeps the architecture
   233→  clean, testable, and consistent with how a multi-user app would work
   234→
   235→## Cross-Cutting Concerns
   236→
   237→### Testing Strategy: Three-Layer Pyramid
   238→
   239→Tests are organized in three layers, from fastest/most-isolated to
   240→slowest/most-integrated. GWT (Given-When-Then) scenarios from the event model
   241→translate directly into test cases.
   242→
   243→**Layer 1 -- Aggregate Unit Tests:**
   244→
   245→Test command handler `handle/2` callbacks in isolation. These verify that valid
   246→inputs produce correct events and that invalid inputs return appropriate errors.
   247→No database, no LiveView, no projections.
   248→
   249→- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** (no PostgreSQL event store)
   250→- Maps directly to **Command slice** GWT scenarios:
   251→  Given = prior events applied to aggregate, When = command dispatched,
   252→  Then = new events emitted (or error returned)
   253→- Files: `test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs`
   254→
   255→**Layer 2 -- Projector / Context Integration Tests:**
   256→
   257→Test projectors and the context module together. Commands dispatch through the
   258→full Commanded pipeline; events are processed by projectors; projections update
   259→in Ecto. Verifies the read model is correct after a sequence of commands.
   260→
   261→- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** for the event store
   262→- Uses **real Ecto / PostgreSQL** for projections (with Ecto sandbox for
   263→  isolation)
   264→- Maps directly to **View slice** GWT scenarios:
   265→  Given = projection state after events, When = new event processed,
   266→  Then = updated projection state
   267→- Files: `test/todo_list/task/projectors/task_projector_test.exs`
   268→
   269→**Layer 3 -- LiveView E2E Tests:**
   270→
   271→Test full LiveView flows: mount the page, simulate user interactions (clicks,
   272→form submissions, drag-and-drop), verify the DOM updates via stream changes.
   273→
   274→- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** for the event store
   275→- Uses **real Ecto / PostgreSQL** for projections (with Ecto sandbox)
   276→- Exercises the entire stack: LiveView -> context -> Commanded -> projector ->
   277→  PubSub -> LiveView stream update
   278→- Files: `test/todo_list_web/live/task_live/index_test.exs`,
   279→  `test/todo_list_web/live/task_live/completed_test.exs`
   280→
   281→**Infrastructure:**
   282→
   283→- **All tests** use Commanded's in-memory adapter (no PostgreSQL event store in
   284→  tests) for speed and isolation
   285→- **Integration and E2E tests** use the real Ecto PostgreSQL database for
   286→  projections, with Ecto sandbox for test isolation
   287→- **No mocking of Ecto Repo** -- integration tests exercise real database
   288→  queries
   289→
   290→### Error Handling: Structured Domain-Typed Errors via newt
   291→
   292→Errors are **first-class domain concepts**, not raw strings or generic tuples.
   293→Each error type is a `newt` domain type with a meaningful name that describes
   294→the business rule violation.
   295→
   296→**Error categories:**
   297→
   298→1. **Validation errors** -- Caught by the boilerplate's `ValidationMiddleware`
   299→   before `handle/2` is called. These originate from `newt` type validation
   300→   (e.g., `TaskDescription.new("")` returns `{:error, reason}`). Returned as
   301→   `{:error, :invalid_command, errors}`.
   302→
   303→2. **Domain errors** -- Returned by `handle/2` when a business rule is
   304→   violated. Each error is a `newt`-based domain type with a semantic name:
   305→
   306→   - `TodoList.TaskAlreadyCompleted` -- completing an already-completed task
   307→   - `TodoList.TaskNotCompleted` -- reopening a task that is not completed
   308→   - `TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged` -- changing description to the same value
   309→   - `TodoList.TaskAlreadyAtPosition` -- reprioritizing to the current position
   310→
   311→   Returned as `{:error, %DomainErrorType{}}` from the command handler.
   312→
   313→3. **System errors** -- Unexpected failures (database connection lost, timeout,
   314→   process crash). These are logged and presented as a generic "something went
   315→   wrong" message in the UI. They do not have dedicated domain types.
   316→
   317→**Error flow:**
   318→
   319→- `handle/2` returns `{:error, %TodoList.TaskAlreadyCompleted{}}` (or similar)
   320→- The context module (`TodoList.Tasks`) translates Commanded dispatch results
   321→  into a consistent format for LiveView
   322→- LiveView pattern-matches on the error type to display appropriate messages
   323→- Tests match on specific error types / atoms, not brittle string comparisons
   324→
   325→**Benefits:**
   326→
   327→- **Testable** -- `assert {:error, %TaskAlreadyCompleted{}} = result`
   328→- **i18n-friendly** -- Error type drives translation key lookup
   329→- **Distinguishable** -- UI can handle validation errors, domain errors, and
   330→  system errors differently
   331→- **Consistent with domain types** -- Errors follow the same `newt` pattern as
   332→  `TaskDescription`, `TaskId`, etc.
   333→
   334→### Warnings as Errors
   335→
   336→Compiler warnings are treated as errors in **all environments** -- dev, test,
   337→and CI. There are no exceptions.
   338→
   339→- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors` must pass at all times
   340→- Enforced in the `mix precommit` alias and in CI pipelines
   341→- Applies to both production code and test code
   342→
   343→**Rationale:** Warnings indicate potential bugs, unclear code, or unused
   344→variables. Allowing warnings to accumulate creates a "broken window" effect
   345→where developers stop paying attention to compiler output. Treating them as
   346→errors from the start prevents this decay and keeps the codebase clean.
   347→
   348→### Typespec Annotations Required
   349→
   350→All public functions **must** have `@spec` annotations -- always, no exceptions.
   351→
   352→**Rules:**
   353→
   354→- **Public functions (`def`):** `@spec` required on every public function
   355→- **Private functions (`defp`):** `@spec` recommended where the types are not
   356→  immediately obvious from the function body
   357→- **Module types:** `@type`, `@typep`, and `@opaque` must be defined for all
   358→  custom data structures (structs, records, complex types)
   359→
   360→**Rationale:** Specs serve as machine-checkable documentation, enable Dialyzer
   361→to catch type errors at compile time, and make the codebase self-documenting.
   362→When combined with `newt` domain types (which have specs built in), Dialyzer
   363→can trace type correctness from command boundaries through aggregates to
   364→projections.
   365→
   366→**Verification:** Dialyzer is used to verify spec correctness. See the quality
   367→gates decision below for how this is integrated into the build process.
   368→
   369→### Pre-commit and CI Quality Gates
   370→
   371→Four mandatory quality gates must pass in both pre-commit hooks and the CI
   372→pipeline. No PR can be merged if any gate fails.
   373→
   374→**Quality gates (in order):**
   375→
   376→1. **Compile** -- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors`
   377→2. **Format** -- `mix format --check-formatted`
   378→3. **Dialyzer** -- `mix dialyzer` (static type analysis, no warnings)
   379→4. **Tests** -- `mix test`
   380→
   381→**Local enforcement:** The existing `mix precommit` alias runs all four checks
   382→in sequence. Developers run this before committing or pushing.
   383→
   384→**CI enforcement:** GitHub Actions runs the same four checks on every PR.
   385→Identical checks locally and in CI ensure no surprises.
   386→
   387→**Dependencies:**
   388→
   389→- `dialyxir` -- Added as a dev/test dependency for Dialyzer integration via
   390→  Mix. Provides `mix dialyzer` task and manages PLT (Persistent Lookup Table)
   391→  caching.
   392→
   393→**Rationale:** Automated quality gates prevent regressions, enforce consistency,
   394→and catch issues before they reach main. Running the same checks in both
   395→pre-commit and CI eliminates "works on my machine" failures.
   396→
   397→### Railway-Oriented Programming with Idiomatic `with`
   398→
   399→The codebase follows **railway-oriented programming** principles (Scott
   400→Wlaschin) using idiomatic Elixir -- no external library required.
   401→
   402→**Standardized result tuple convention** across the entire codebase:
   403→
   404→- `:ok` -- success with no data
   405→- `{:ok, data}` -- success with data
   406→- `{:error, %DomainErrorType{}}` -- failure with a domain-typed error (newt
   407→  types per the error handling decision)
   408→
   409→**Elixir's `with` statement IS railway-oriented programming.** It pattern
   410→matches on `{:ok, value}` at each step and short-circuits on mismatch --
   411→exactly the "two-track" model where the happy path flows forward and errors
   412→divert to the error track.
   413→
   414→**Usage rules:**
   415→
   416→- All public API functions in the context module (`TodoList.Tasks`) return
   417→  result tuples
   418→- Command `handle/2` callbacks return `{:ok, [events]}` or
   419→  `{:error, %DomainErrorType{}}`
   420→- Domain type constructors (`Newt.new/1`) return `{:ok, wrapped}` or
   421→  `{:error, reason}`
   422→- `with` blocks **must** have explicit `else` clauses that pattern match on
   423→  specific error types -- no catch-all `_ ->` clauses
   424→
   425→**Rationale:** Elixir's `with` provides the same short-circuit semantics as
   426→dedicated railway libraries without adding a dependency or custom operators
   427→that may confuse newcomers. The standardized tuple convention makes error
   428→propagation predictable and composable across all layers of the application.
   429→

Create the test file `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description/test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs`.

This tests the ChangeTaskDescription command through the full Commanded dispatch pipeline (in-memory event store, validation middleware, authorization middleware, aggregate).

## Key Setup Facts:

- Commanded app: `TodoList.App`
- In-memory event store, configured in `config/test.exs`
- Reset between tests: `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)`
- Use `async: false` since the in-memory store is shared state
- Commands are dispatched via `TodoList.App.dispatch/1`
- Commands require an `auth_subject` field — pass `nil` for this single-user app
- Command fields use Ecto types, so raw values (strings, integers) are passed and the middleware validates/casts them

## Command Structs:

```elixir
# AddTask (for test setup)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "some-uuidv7",
  description: "some description",
  position: 1
}

# ChangeTaskDescription (the command under test)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "same-uuidv7",
  new_description: "new description"
}
```

## Generate UUIDv7 IDs:
Use `UUIDv7.generate()` to create valid UUIDv7 strings.

## 4 Test Scenarios (ONE assertion per test):

### Scenario 1: Change a task's description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 2: Reject changing to same description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

### Scenario 3: Change after previous change succeeds
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office at 2pm"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 4: Reject changing back to current (after previous change)
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office" 
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

## Template:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.AggregateTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false

  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask
  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription

  setup do
    Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)
    :ok
  end

  describe "ChangeTaskDescription" do
    test "changes a task's description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to the same description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end

    test "changes description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to current description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end
  end
end
```

Create this test file. Make sure the directory `test/todo_list/task/` exists.

After creating, run the tests with:
```
cd /home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description && MIX_TEST_PARTITION=5 mix test test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs --trace 2>&1
```

- In-memory event store, configured in `config/test.exs`
- Reset between tests: `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)`
- Use `async: false` since the in-memory store is shared state
- Commands are dispatched via `TodoList.App.dispatch/1`
- Commands require an `auth_subject` field — pass `nil` for this single-user app
- Command fields use Ecto types, so raw values (strings, integers) are passed and the middleware validates/casts them

## Command Structs:

```elixir
# AddTask (for test setup)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "some-uuidv7",
  description: "some description",
  position: 1
}

# ChangeTaskDescription (the command under test)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "same-uuidv7",
  new_description: "new description"
}
```

## Generate UUIDv7 IDs:
Use `UUIDv7.generate()` to create valid UUIDv7 strings.

## 4 Test Scenarios (ONE assertion per test):

### Scenario 1: Change a task's description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 2: Reject changing to same description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

### Scenario 3: Change after previous change succeeds
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office at 2pm"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 4: Reject changing back to current (after previous change)
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office" 
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

## Template:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.AggregateTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false

  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask
  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription

  setup do
    Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)
    :ok
  end

  describe "ChangeTaskDescription" do
    test "changes a task's description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to the same description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end

    test "changes description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to current description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end
  end
end
```

```
cd /home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description && MIX_TEST_PARTITION=5 mix test test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs --trace 2>&1
```

This tests the ChangeTaskDescription command through the full Commanded dispatch pipeline (in-memory event store, validation middleware, authorization middleware, aggregate).

## Key Setup Facts:

- Commanded app: `TodoList.App`
- In-memory event store, configured in `config/test.exs`
- Reset between tests: `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)`
- Use `async: false` since the in-memory store is shared state
- Commands are dispatched via `TodoList.App.dispatch/1`
- Commands require an `auth_subject` field — pass `nil` for this single-user app
- Command fields use Ecto types, so raw values (strings, integers) are passed and the middleware validates/casts them

## Command Structs:

```elixir
# AddTask (for test setup)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "some-uuidv7",
  description: "some description",
  position: 1
}

# ChangeTaskDescription (the command under test)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "same-uuidv7",
  new_description: "new description"
}
```

## Generate UUIDv7 IDs:
Use `UUIDv7.generate()` to create valid UUIDv7 strings.

## 4 Test Scenarios (ONE assertion per test):

### Scenario 1: Change a task's description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 2: Reject changing to same description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

### Scenario 3: Change after previous change succeeds
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office at 2pm"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 4: Reject changing back to current (after previous change)
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office" 
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

## Template:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.AggregateTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false

  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask
  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription

  setup do
    Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)
    :ok
  end

  describe "ChangeTaskDescription" do
    test "changes a task's description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to the same description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end

    test "changes description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to current description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end
  end
end
```
```

```

defmodule TodoList.Task.AggregateTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false

  alias Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubject
  alias TodoList.App
  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.{AddTask, ChangeTaskDescription}
  alias TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged

  setup do
    {:ok, _} = Application.ensure_all_started(:todo_list)
    :ok
  end

  describe "ChangeTaskDescription" do
    test "changes a task's description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      assert :ok =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist office"
               })
    end

    test "rejects changing to the same description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      assert {:error, %DescriptionUnchanged{}} =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist"
               })
    end

    test "changes description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          new_description: "Call the dentist office"
        })

      assert :ok =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm"
               })
    end

    test "rejects changing to current description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          new_description: "Call the dentist office"
        })

      assert {:error, %DescriptionUnchanged{}} =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist office"
               })
    end
  end
end

You are working in `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description`.

Create the test file `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description/test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs`.

This tests the ChangeTaskDescription command through the full Commanded dispatch pipeline (in-memory event store, validation middleware, authorization middleware, aggregate).

## Key Setup Facts:

- Commanded app: `TodoList.App`
- In-memory event store, configured in `config/test.exs`
- Reset between tests: `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)`
- Use `async: false` since the in-memory store is shared state
- Commands are dispatched via `TodoList.App.dispatch/1`
- Commands require an `auth_subject` field — pass `nil` for this single-user app
- Command fields use Ecto types, so raw values (strings, integers) are passed and the middleware validates/casts them

## Command Structs:

```elixir
# AddTask (for test setup)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "some-uuidv7",
  description: "some description",
  position: 1
}

# ChangeTaskDescription (the command under test)
%TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription{
  auth_subject: nil,
  task_id: "same-uuidv7",
  new_description: "new description"
}
```

## Generate UUIDv7 IDs:
Use `UUIDv7.generate()` to create valid UUIDv7 strings.

## 4 Test Scenarios (ONE assertion per test):

### Scenario 1: Change a task's description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 2: Reject changing to same description
**Given**: AddTask dispatched with description "Call the dentist"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

### Scenario 3: Change after previous change succeeds
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office"
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office at 2pm"
**Then**: Returns `:ok`

### Scenario 4: Reject changing back to current (after previous change)
**Given**: AddTask dispatched, then ChangeTaskDescription to "Call the dentist office" 
**When**: ChangeTaskDescription dispatched with new_description "Call the dentist office"
**Then**: Returns `{:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}`

## Template:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.AggregateTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false

  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask
  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription

  setup do
    Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory.reset!(TodoList.App)
    :ok
  end

  describe "ChangeTaskDescription" do
    test "changes a task's description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to the same description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end

    test "changes description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm"})

      assert result == :ok
    end

    test "rejects changing to current description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%AddTask{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, description: "Call the dentist", position: 1})
      :ok = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      result = TodoList.App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{auth_subject: nil, task_id: task_id, new_description: "Call the dentist office"})

      assert result == {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    end
  end
end
```

Create this test file. Make sure the directory `test/todo_list/task/` exists.

After creating, run the tests with:
```
cd /home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description && MIX_TEST_PARTITION=5 mix test test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs --trace 2>&1
```

content.text:
The complete-task worktree doesn't have `ChangeTaskDescription` -- it only has `AddTask` and `CompleteTask`. The change-description worktree has a divergent version of `AddTask` that uses `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1` in `handle/2`, which fails because the validation middleware hasn't cast the raw strings into newt types.

These are properly failing tests. The tests are written correctly -- they follow the established patterns from the working complete-task worktree (using `AuthSubject.system_user()`, `Application.ensure_all_started/1`, raw string values for command fields). The failures are in the production code, which is exactly what RED phase tests should expose.

Here is the summary:

---

**Test file created:** `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description/test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs`

**4 tests written, all 4 failing:**

1. **`changes a task's description`** (L15) -- Fails with `MatchError` on AddTask dispatch
2. **`rejects changing to the same description`** (L34) -- Fails with `MatchError` on AddTask dispatch
3. **`changes description after a previous change`** (L53) -- Fails with `MatchError` on AddTask dispatch
4. **`rejects changing to current description after a previous change`** (L79) -- Fails with `MatchError` on AddTask dispatch

**Root cause of all failures:** The `AddTask.handle/2` callback on this branch calls `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap(command.task_id)` (and similarly for description and position), but the command fields are still raw strings/integers at that point -- the `Newt.Unwrap` protocol is not implemented for `BitString`. The validation middleware should be casting these into newt types, but something in the pipeline is not casting correctly, or `handle/2` should not be calling `unwrap/1` (as is the case on the complete-task worktree where `AddTask.handle/2` uses the command fields directly without unwrapping).

**Note for GREEN phase:** There are two issues to resolve:
1. Fix `AddTask.handle/2` to not call `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1` on command fields (align with the pattern used in the complete-task worktree), OR ensure the middleware properly casts the fields before `handle/2` is called.
2. Verify that `ChangeTaskDescription.handle/2` (which also calls `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1`) works correctly after the casting pipeline is fixed.

**Ready for:** `sdlc:domain` review, then `sdlc:green` implementation.

    70→

    72→               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{

    74→                 task_id: task_id,

    76→               })
    77→    end

    80→      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()
    81→
    82→      :ok =
    83→        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
    84→          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
    85→          task_id: task_id,
    86→          description: "Call the dentist",
    87→          position: 1
    88→        })

    94→          new_description: "Call the dentist office"
    95→        })

defmodule TodoList.Task.AggregateTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false

  alias Commanded.Boilerplate.AuthSubject
  alias TodoList.App
  alias TodoList.Task.Commands.{AddTask, ChangeTaskDescription}
  alias TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged

  setup do
    {:ok, _} = Application.ensure_all_started(:todo_list)
    :ok
  end

  describe "ChangeTaskDescription" do
    test "changes a task's description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      assert :ok =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist office"
               })
    end

    test "rejects changing to the same description" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      assert {:error, %DescriptionUnchanged{}} =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist"
               })
    end

    test "changes description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          new_description: "Call the dentist office"
        })

      assert :ok =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist office at 2pm"
               })
    end

    test "rejects changing to current description after a previous change" do
      task_id = UUIDv7.generate()

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%AddTask{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          description: "Call the dentist",
          position: 1
        })

      :ok =
        App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
          auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
          task_id: task_id,
          new_description: "Call the dentist office"
        })

      assert {:error, %DescriptionUnchanged{}} =
               App.dispatch(%ChangeTaskDescription{
                 auth_subject: AuthSubject.system_user(),
                 task_id: task_id,
                 new_description: "Call the dentist office"
               })
    end
  end
end

content.text: Good tests. The RED agent used `AuthSubject.system_user()` instead of `nil` — smarter than what I suggested. Now the GREEN phase: fix both command handlers to stop calling `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1` since the command fields are raw values, not Newt structs.

input.prompt:
You are working in `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description`.

Fix `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description/lib/todo_list/task/commands/add_task.ex`.

The current `handle/2` calls `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1` on command fields, but command fields are raw values (strings, integers), NOT Newt structs. The validation middleware validates but doesn't wrap the values. So `unwrap/1` fails with a protocol error.

Remove all `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap()` calls and use command fields directly:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask do
  @moduledoc "Command to add a new task."

  use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command, identifier: :task_id

  alias TodoList.Task.Events.TaskAdded

  inputs do
    field :task_id, TodoList.TaskId.Ectotype.t()
    field :description, TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype.t()
    field :position, TodoList.TaskPosition.Ectotype.t()
  end

  aggregate do
  end

  @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
  def handle(_aggregate, command) do
    {:ok,
     [
       %TaskAdded{
         task_id: command.task_id,
         description: command.description,
         position: command.position
       }
     ]}
  end

  @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
  def authorize(_command), do: :ok
end
```

content.content:
1→# Architecture
     2→
     3→> This is the authoritative source for the current architecture of the Todo List application.
     4→> For the rationale behind each decision, see the ADR PRs labeled `adr` on GitHub.
     5→
     6→## Technology Stack
     7→
     8→### Event Sourcing Framework: Commanded
     9→
    10→The application uses [Commanded](https://github.com/commanded/commanded) as its
    11→CQRS/ES framework. Commanded provides the full command dispatch pipeline,
    12→aggregate lifecycle management, event persistence, and projection infrastructure.
    13→
    14→Key components from the Commanded ecosystem:
    15→
    16→- **commanded** -- Core framework (command router, aggregate callbacks, middleware)
    17→- **commanded_eventstore_adapter** -- Bridges Commanded to the EventStore backend
    18→- **commanded_ecto_projections** -- Ecto-based read model projections
    19→- **commanded_boilerplate** -- Ergonomic macros for aggregate-per-command pattern
    20→  (GitHub: `jwilger/commanded_boilerplate`)
    21→
    22→See "Domain Architecture" below for how commands and aggregates are structured.
    23→
    24→### Event Store Backend: PostgreSQL (EventStore) + In-Memory for Tests
    25→
    26→Events are durably stored in PostgreSQL using the
    27→[eventstore](https://github.com/commanded/eventstore) library with the
    28→`commanded_eventstore_adapter`. This requires a dedicated PostgreSQL database
    29→separate from the main Ecto application database.
    30→
    31→**Two PostgreSQL databases** (same docker-compose instance):
    32→
    33→- **Application database** (Ecto) -- Projections / read models, standard Phoenix data
    34→- **Event store database** (EventStore) -- Append-only event streams
    35→
    36→**Test environment:** Tests use Commanded's built-in in-memory event store adapter.
    37→This keeps tests fast (no database I/O), isolated (no shared state between tests),
    38→and free of infrastructure dependencies.
    39→
    40→### Projection Mechanism: commanded_ecto_projections
    41→
    42→Read models are built using
    43→[commanded_ecto_projections](https://github.com/commanded/commanded-ecto-projections).
    44→Each read model has a corresponding Projector module that uses
    45→`Commanded.Projections.Ecto` and implements `project/2` callbacks for each event
    46→type it handles.
    47→
    48→**Two projectors:**
    49→
    50→- **TaskProjector** -- Projects events into an Ecto schema used by both the
    51→  ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList read models
    52→
    53→Projection tables live in the **application database** (Ecto), not the event store
    54→database. The library handles:
    55→
    56→- **Idempotency** -- Tracks which events have been processed, preventing duplicates
    57→- **Error handling** -- Provides configurable error/retry behaviour
    58→- **Subscription management** -- Integrates with Commanded's event subscription system
    59→
    60→### UUID Generation: UUIDv7 (uuidv7 hex package)
    61→
    62→Task identifiers use UUID v7 via the
    63→[uuidv7](https://hex.pm/packages/uuidv7) hex package (v1.0.0). UUID v7
    64→(RFC 9562) embeds a Unix timestamp in the high bits, producing identifiers that
    65→are monotonically increasing and time-sortable while remaining globally unique.
    66→
    67→The package includes an `UUIDv7` Ecto type for seamless schema integration.
    68→
    69→**Generation strategy:** Task IDs are generated by the context module
    70→(`TodoList.Tasks`) and passed on the command struct. The aggregate does not
    71→generate IDs internally. This design enables tests to supply deterministic IDs
    72→without mocking.
    73→
    74→## Domain Architecture
    75→
    76→### Aggregate Design: Aggregate-per-Command with commanded_boilerplate
    77→
    78→The application uses the **aggregate-per-command** pattern via the
    79→[commanded_boilerplate](https://github.com/jwilger/commanded_boilerplate) library.
    80→Each command module defines its own inline aggregate containing **only** the state
    81→fields needed for that specific command's decision-making. There is no shared
    82→"Task" aggregate module.
    83→
    84→**Key aspects:**
    85→
    86→- `use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command, identifier: :task_id` -- Sets up the command
    87→  module with routing, validation middleware, and authorization middleware
    88→- `inputs do ... end` -- Defines the command's input fields as a typed struct
    89→- `aggregate do ... end` -- Defines the command's inline aggregate with only
    90→  relevant fields and `apply/2` callbacks for event application
    91→- `handle/2` -- The command handler callback that receives aggregate state and
    92→  command, returns events or errors
    93→- `register_command/2` -- Auto-registers the command with Commanded's router
    94→
    95→**Domain logic** lives directly in the command module's `handle/2` callback.
    96→There is no separate domain layer and no `lib/todo_list/domain/` directory.
    97→
    98→**Aggregate lifecycle:** `DefaultLifespan` stops aggregate processes immediately
    99→after command execution (reliability over performance). Each command execution
   100→loads only the state it needs.
   101→
   102→**Authorization:** The boilerplate includes authorization middleware. For this
   103→single-user app with no authentication, the `authorize/2` callback returns `:ok`
   104→unconditionally.
   105→
   106→**Read model queries:** The boilerplate also provides `Commanded.Boilerplate.Query`
   107→for read model access with validation and Repo integration.
   108→
   109→**Maintenance:** The `commanded_boilerplate` library is owned by the project
   110→maintainer. If compatibility issues arise with newer Commanded versions, it can be
   111→cloned locally at `../commanded_boilerplate/` for updates.
   112→
   113→### Domain Types: Semantic Types via newt
   114→
   115→The application uses **dedicated semantic domain types** built with the
   116→[newt](https://hex.pm/packages/newt) library (v9.0.0). Newt is already a
   117→transitive dependency via `commanded_boilerplate`; the library is owned by the
   118→project maintainer (GitHub: `jwilger/newt`).
   119→
   120→**Naming convention -- domain-semantic, not structural:** Types are named after
   121→what they represent in the domain, not their structural shape.
   122→
   123→- `TodoList.TaskDescription` -- wraps `String.t()`, validates non-empty /
   124→  non-blank, trims whitespace
   125→- `TodoList.TaskId` -- wraps `String.t()`, validates UUIDv7 format
   126→- `TodoList.TaskPosition` -- wraps `integer()`, validates positive integer
   127→
   128→**How newt works:**
   129→
   130→- `use Newt, type: <inner_type>` -- Creates a wrapped struct with `new/1`,
   131→  `new!/1`, `unwrap/1`
   132→- `validate/1` callback -- Override to add domain-specific validation logic
   133→- Auto-generated `Ectotype` module (e.g., `TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype`)
   134→  implementing `Ecto.Type` for seamless Ecto schema integration
   135→- Protocol implementations for `Jason`, `Phoenix.Param`, `Phoenix.HTML.Safe`,
   136→  `Inspect`, `String.Chars`
   137→- `type!/2` guard macro for pattern matching
   138→
   139→**Parse at boundaries, trust internally:** Raw strings and integers enter the
   140→system at command boundaries (e.g., from LiveView forms). Commands parse them
   141→into domain types using `new/1`. Once parsed, domain types flow through events,
   142→aggregates, and projections without re-validation. This ensures invalid data
   143→never propagates past the boundary.
   144→
   145→**Ecto integration:** Each newt type auto-generates an `Ectotype` module that
   146→implements `Ecto.Type`, so projection schemas use field types like
   147→`TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype` directly.
   148→
   149→**Maintenance:** The `newt` library is owned by the project maintainer. If
   150→issues arise, it can be cloned locally at `../newt/` for updates.
   151→
   152→### Context Module API: TodoList.Tasks
   153→
   154→The context module `TodoList.Tasks` is the **single API boundary** between the
   155→web layer (LiveView) and the domain layer (commands, aggregates, projections).
   156→LiveView ONLY talks to the context module -- never to Commanded directly.
   157→
   158→**Command functions** wrap command dispatch. Each function constructs the command
   159→struct, dispatches it via Commanded, and returns a clean result:
   160→
   161→- `add_task(description)` -- Generates a `TaskId` via UUIDv7, builds an
   162→  `AddTask` command, dispatches it. Returns `{:ok, task_id}` or
   163→  `{:error, reason}`.
   164→- `complete_task(task_id)` -- Returns `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`.
   165→- `reopen_task(task_id)` -- Returns `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`.
   166→- `change_task_description(task_id, new_description)` -- Returns `:ok` or
   167→  `{:error, reason}`.
   168→- `reprioritize_task(task_id, new_position)` -- Returns `:ok` or
   169→  `{:error, reason}`.
   170→- `delete_task(task_id)` -- Returns `:ok` or `{:error, reason}`.
   171→
   172→**Query functions** wrap read model access using
   173→`Commanded.Boilerplate.Query` internally:
   174→
   175→- `active_tasks()` -- Returns the list of active (non-completed) tasks ordered
   176→  by position.
   177→- `completed_tasks()` -- Returns the list of completed tasks ordered by
   178→  completion time descending.
   179→
   180→**Responsibilities of the context module:**
   181→
   182→- **UUID generation** -- Creates `TaskId` values via UUIDv7 for new tasks
   183→- **Command construction** -- Builds command structs from function arguments
   184→- **Command dispatch** -- Sends commands through Commanded's pipeline
   185→- **Response transformation** -- Converts Commanded dispatch results into clean
   186→  `{:ok, result}` / `{:error, reason}` tuples
   187→- **Query execution** -- Fetches projection data via the boilerplate's Query
   188→  module
   189→
   190→**Testing benefit:** LiveView tests can mock or stub the context module rather
   191→than mocking Commanded internals, keeping integration tests focused on UI
   192→behaviour.
   193→
   194→## Integration Patterns
   195→
   196→### LiveView Integration: Streams + Projection Subscriptions (Hybrid)
   197→
   198→LiveView uses a **hybrid** approach: **streams** for the main task lists and
   199→**direct reads** for one-off queries or detail views.
   200→
   201→**Streams for lists:** The ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList are rendered
   202→using LiveView's `stream/3`. Streams efficiently track insertions, updates, and
   203→deletions -- only changed items are sent to the client.
   204→
   205→- Parent elements use `phx-update="stream"`
   206→- Each streamed item has a DOM ID (e.g., `"tasks-#{task.id}"`)
   207→- Streams are **not enumerable** -- empty states and counts use separate assigns
   208→
   209→**Projection subscriptions for real-time updates:** Projectors broadcast
   210→changes via Phoenix PubSub after processing events. LiveView subscribes to
   211→these broadcasts on mount and updates streams accordingly.
   212→
   213→**Lifecycle:**
   214→
   215→1. **Mount:** Subscribe to projection update topics via Phoenix PubSub; stream
   216→   initial data from `TodoList.Tasks.active_tasks()` or
   217→   `TodoList.Tasks.completed_tasks()`
   218→2. **User action:** Event handler calls a context command function (e.g.,
   219→   `TodoList.Tasks.complete_task(task_id)`)
   220→3. **Projection update:** The projector processes the resulting event, updates
   221→   the database, and broadcasts via PubSub
   222→4. **Stream update:** LiveView receives the PubSub message in `handle_info/2`
   223→   and calls `stream_insert/4`, `stream_delete/3`, etc.
   224→
   225→**Key details:**
   226→
   227→- Commands dispatch through the context module (never directly from LiveView)
   228→- The LiveView does **not** re-fetch the full list after each command; the
   229→  PubSub subscription delivers granular updates
   230→- For empty states and aggregate counts (e.g., `completed_count`), use separate
   231→  assigns updated alongside stream operations
   232→- This is a single-user app, but the subscription pattern keeps the architecture
   233→  clean, testable, and consistent with how a multi-user app would work
   234→
   235→## Cross-Cutting Concerns
   236→
   237→### Testing Strategy: Three-Layer Pyramid
   238→
   239→Tests are organized in three layers, from fastest/most-isolated to
   240→slowest/most-integrated. GWT (Given-When-Then) scenarios from the event model
   241→translate directly into test cases.
   242→
   243→**Layer 1 -- Aggregate Unit Tests:**
   244→
   245→Test command handler `handle/2` callbacks in isolation. These verify that valid
   246→inputs produce correct events and that invalid inputs return appropriate errors.
   247→No database, no LiveView, no projections.
   248→
   249→- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** (no PostgreSQL event store)
   250→- Maps directly to **Command slice** GWT scenarios:
   251→  Given = prior events applied to aggregate, When = command dispatched,
   252→  Then = new events emitted (or error returned)
   253→- Files: `test/todo_list/task/aggregate_test.exs`
   254→
   255→**Layer 2 -- Projector / Context Integration Tests:**
   256→
   257→Test projectors and the context module together. Commands dispatch through the
   258→full Commanded pipeline; events are processed by projectors; projections update
   259→in Ecto. Verifies the read model is correct after a sequence of commands.
   260→
   261→- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** for the event store
   262→- Uses **real Ecto / PostgreSQL** for projections (with Ecto sandbox for
   263→  isolation)
   264→- Maps directly to **View slice** GWT scenarios:
   265→  Given = projection state after events, When = new event processed,
   266→  Then = updated projection state
   267→- Files: `test/todo_list/task/projectors/task_projector_test.exs`
   268→
   269→**Layer 3 -- LiveView E2E Tests:**
   270→
   271→Test full LiveView flows: mount the page, simulate user interactions (clicks,
   272→form submissions, drag-and-drop), verify the DOM updates via stream changes.
   273→
   274→- Uses Commanded's **in-memory adapter** for the event store
   275→- Uses **real Ecto / PostgreSQL** for projections (with Ecto sandbox)
   276→- Exercises the entire stack: LiveView -> context -> Commanded -> projector ->
   277→  PubSub -> LiveView stream update
   278→- Files: `test/todo_list_web/live/task_live/index_test.exs`,
   279→  `test/todo_list_web/live/task_live/completed_test.exs`
   280→
   281→**Infrastructure:**
   282→
   283→- **All tests** use Commanded's in-memory adapter (no PostgreSQL event store in
   284→  tests) for speed and isolation
   285→- **Integration and E2E tests** use the real Ecto PostgreSQL database for
   286→  projections, with Ecto sandbox for test isolation
   287→- **No mocking of Ecto Repo** -- integration tests exercise real database
   288→  queries
   289→
   290→### Error Handling: Structured Domain-Typed Errors via newt
   291→
   292→Errors are **first-class domain concepts**, not raw strings or generic tuples.
   293→Each error type is a `newt` domain type with a meaningful name that describes
   294→the business rule violation.
   295→
   296→**Error categories:**
   297→
   298→1. **Validation errors** -- Caught by the boilerplate's `ValidationMiddleware`
   299→   before `handle/2` is called. These originate from `newt` type validation
   300→   (e.g., `TaskDescription.new("")` returns `{:error, reason}`). Returned as
   301→   `{:error, :invalid_command, errors}`.
   302→
   303→2. **Domain errors** -- Returned by `handle/2` when a business rule is
   304→   violated. Each error is a `newt`-based domain type with a semantic name:
   305→
   306→   - `TodoList.TaskAlreadyCompleted` -- completing an already-completed task
   307→   - `TodoList.TaskNotCompleted` -- reopening a task that is not completed
   308→   - `TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged` -- changing description to the same value
   309→   - `TodoList.TaskAlreadyAtPosition` -- reprioritizing to the current position
   310→
   311→   Returned as `{:error, %DomainErrorType{}}` from the command handler.
   312→
   313→3. **System errors** -- Unexpected failures (database connection lost, timeout,
   314→   process crash). These are logged and presented as a generic "something went
   315→   wrong" message in the UI. They do not have dedicated domain types.
   316→
   317→**Error flow:**
   318→
   319→- `handle/2` returns `{:error, %TodoList.TaskAlreadyCompleted{}}` (or similar)
   320→- The context module (`TodoList.Tasks`) translates Commanded dispatch results
   321→  into a consistent format for LiveView
   322→- LiveView pattern-matches on the error type to display appropriate messages
   323→- Tests match on specific error types / atoms, not brittle string comparisons
   324→
   325→**Benefits:**
   326→
   327→- **Testable** -- `assert {:error, %TaskAlreadyCompleted{}} = result`
   328→- **i18n-friendly** -- Error type drives translation key lookup
   329→- **Distinguishable** -- UI can handle validation errors, domain errors, and
   330→  system errors differently
   331→- **Consistent with domain types** -- Errors follow the same `newt` pattern as
   332→  `TaskDescription`, `TaskId`, etc.
   333→
   334→### Warnings as Errors
   335→
   336→Compiler warnings are treated as errors in **all environments** -- dev, test,
   337→and CI. There are no exceptions.
   338→
   339→- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors` must pass at all times
   340→- Enforced in the `mix precommit` alias and in CI pipelines
   341→- Applies to both production code and test code
   342→
   343→**Rationale:** Warnings indicate potential bugs, unclear code, or unused
   344→variables. Allowing warnings to accumulate creates a "broken window" effect
   345→where developers stop paying attention to compiler output. Treating them as
   346→errors from the start prevents this decay and keeps the codebase clean.
   347→
   348→### Typespec Annotations Required
   349→
   350→All public functions **must** have `@spec` annotations -- always, no exceptions.
   351→
   352→**Rules:**
   353→
   354→- **Public functions (`def`):** `@spec` required on every public function
   355→- **Private functions (`defp`):** `@spec` recommended where the types are not
   356→  immediately obvious from the function body
   357→- **Module types:** `@type`, `@typep`, and `@opaque` must be defined for all
   358→  custom data structures (structs, records, complex types)
   359→
   360→**Rationale:** Specs serve as machine-checkable documentation, enable Dialyzer
   361→to catch type errors at compile time, and make the codebase self-documenting.
   362→When combined with `newt` domain types (which have specs built in), Dialyzer
   363→can trace type correctness from command boundaries through aggregates to
   364→projections.
   365→
   366→**Verification:** Dialyzer is used to verify spec correctness. See the quality
   367→gates decision below for how this is integrated into the build process.
   368→
   369→### Pre-commit and CI Quality Gates
   370→
   371→Four mandatory quality gates must pass in both pre-commit hooks and the CI
   372→pipeline. No PR can be merged if any gate fails.
   373→
   374→**Quality gates (in order):**
   375→
   376→1. **Compile** -- `mix compile --warnings-as-errors`
   377→2. **Format** -- `mix format --check-formatted`
   378→3. **Dialyzer** -- `mix dialyzer` (static type analysis, no warnings)
   379→4. **Tests** -- `mix test`
   380→
   381→**Local enforcement:** The existing `mix precommit` alias runs all four checks
   382→in sequence. Developers run this before committing or pushing.
   383→
   384→**CI enforcement:** GitHub Actions runs the same four checks on every PR.
   385→Identical checks locally and in CI ensure no surprises.
   386→
   387→**Dependencies:**
   388→
   389→- `dialyxir` -- Added as a dev/test dependency for Dialyzer integration via
   390→  Mix. Provides `mix dialyzer` task and manages PLT (Persistent Lookup Table)
   391→  caching.
   392→
   393→**Rationale:** Automated quality gates prevent regressions, enforce consistency,
   394→and catch issues before they reach main. Running the same checks in both
   395→pre-commit and CI eliminates "works on my machine" failures.
   396→
   397→### Railway-Oriented Programming with Idiomatic `with`
   398→
   399→The codebase follows **railway-oriented programming** principles (Scott
   400→Wlaschin) using idiomatic Elixir -- no external library required.
   401→
   402→**Standardized result tuple convention** across the entire codebase:
   403→
   404→- `:ok` -- success with no data
   405→- `{:ok, data}` -- success with data
   406→- `{:error, %DomainErrorType{}}` -- failure with a domain-typed error (newt
   407→  types per the error handling decision)
   408→
   409→**Elixir's `with` statement IS railway-oriented programming.** It pattern
   410→matches on `{:ok, value}` at each step and short-circuits on mismatch --
   411→exactly the "two-track" model where the happy path flows forward and errors
   412→divert to the error track.
   413→
   414→**Usage rules:**
   415→
   416→- All public API functions in the context module (`TodoList.Tasks`) return
   417→  result tuples
   418→- Command `handle/2` callbacks return `{:ok, [events]}` or
   419→  `{:error, %DomainErrorType{}}`
   420→- Domain type constructors (`Newt.new/1`) return `{:ok, wrapped}` or
   421→  `{:error, reason}`
   422→- `with` blocks **must** have explicit `else` clauses that pattern match on
   423→  specific error types -- no catch-all `_ ->` clauses
   424→
   425→**Rationale:** Elixir's `with` provides the same short-circuit semantics as
   426→dedicated railway libraries without adding a dependency or custom operators
   427→that may confuse newcomers. The standardized tuple convention makes error
   428→propagation predictable and composable across all layers of the application.
   429→

You are working in `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description`.

Fix `/home/jwilger/projects/todo_list-worktrees/change-description/lib/todo_list/task/commands/add_task.ex`.

The current `handle/2` calls `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1` on command fields, but command fields are raw values (strings, integers), NOT Newt structs. The validation middleware validates but doesn't wrap the values. So `unwrap/1` fails with a protocol error.

Remove all `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap()` calls and use command fields directly:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask do
  @moduledoc "Command to add a new task."

  use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command, identifier: :task_id

  alias TodoList.Task.Events.TaskAdded

  inputs do
    field :task_id, TodoList.TaskId.Ectotype.t()
    field :description, TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype.t()
    field :position, TodoList.TaskPosition.Ectotype.t()
  end

  aggregate do
  end

  @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
  def handle(_aggregate, command) do
    {:ok,
     [
       %TaskAdded{
         task_id: command.task_id,
         description: command.description,
         position: command.position
       }
     ]}
  end

  @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
  def authorize(_command), do: :ok
end
```

The current `handle/2` calls `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap/1` on command fields, but command fields are raw values (strings), NOT Newt structs. The validation middleware validates but doesn't wrap the values. So `unwrap/1` fails with a protocol error.

Remove all `Newt.Unwrap.unwrap()` calls and use command fields directly:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.Commands.ChangeTaskDescription do
  @moduledoc "Command to change an existing task's description."

  use Commanded.Boilerplate.Command, identifier: :task_id

  alias TodoList.Task.Events.TaskAdded
  alias TodoList.Task.Events.TaskDescriptionChanged

  inputs do
    field :task_id, TodoList.TaskId.Ectotype.t()
    field :new_description, TodoList.TaskDescription.Ectotype.t()
  end

  aggregate do
    field :description, :string, default: nil

    @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Aggregate
    def apply(state, %TaskAdded{description: desc}) do
      %{state | description: desc}
    end

    @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Aggregate
    def apply(state, %TaskDescriptionChanged{description: desc}) do
      %{state | description: desc}
    end
  end

  @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
  def handle(aggregate, command) do
    if aggregate.description == command.new_description do
      {:error, %TodoList.DescriptionUnchanged{}}
    else
      {:ok,
       [
         %TaskDescriptionChanged{
           task_id: command.task_id,
           description: command.new_description
         }
       ]}
    end
  end

  @impl Commanded.Boilerplate.Command
  def authorize(_command), do: :ok
end
```
